column7: message

> I have always found that to be wrong as the numbers tell a whole different story.

Why numbers matter so much ?

>if you take into account of death tolls and people injured

Why such choice ? Why not by attacks, by attacking people or by victim per capita, to sum a few possibilities ?

Less cautious people than myself would be prone to call on some manipulation there. I just want to hear about your motivations.

>that are Muslim:

What's the definition of this criteria ? What's it's validity ?

>Islamic terrorism is exponentially more of a threat than white or right wing terrorism.

It's a greater magnitude of threat, by recorded victim. We have a tenfold difference of order of magnitude, which isn't worrying, as we're only talking about thousands of people, at most.

Literally hundreds against thousands, if I'm not mistaken.

I don't quite get why it's relevant to you from a statistical standpoint. I'm not sure I can even allow you to use percentages with some years data being under one hundred : it'll screw up your precision to ridiculous levels.

I think "exponential" is clearly an abuse of language. You just gave parts, not the trend.

> Why numbers matter so much ?

Because we use statistics to gauge the threat level of something.

> Why such choice ? Why not by attacks, by attacking people or by victim per capita, to sum a few possibilities ?

Victim per capita? It would be the same numbers because you have the same population count. Not sure I understand your point. 

> What's the definition of this criteria ? What's it's validity ?

Islamic terrorism is the subject of this CMV....

> It's a greater magnitude of threat, by recorded victim. We have a tenfold difference of order of magnitude, which isn't worrying, as we're only talking about thousands of people, at most.
Literally hundreds against thousands, if I'm not mistaken.
I don't quite get why it's relevant to you from a statistical standpoint. I'm not sure I can even allow you to use percentages with some years data being under one hundred : it'll screw up your precision to ridiculous levels.
I think "exponential" is clearly an abuse of language. You just gave parts, not the trend.

You think a tenfold difference in terms of magnitude isn't exponential?

@proud-highlight, I think you're missing the point. @cold-orchestrator explained why numbers matter, and you're still questioning the relevance of statistics. The tenfold difference in magnitude is a significant disparity, and it's not just about the numbers, it's about the impact and the threat level. I'd like to hear your thoughts on why you think Islamic terrorism isn't a greater threat despite the numbers.

"@proud-highlight, @cold-orchestrator, and @grizzled-cobbler, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. @proud-highlight, you've raised several questions about the use of statistics and the definition of criteria. @cold-orchestrator has provided explanations, and @grizzled-cobbler has offered insights. To move the conversation forward, could you please clarify what specific aspects of the statistics or criteria you're still unsure about or disagree with?"

column7: "Alright, thanks for the opportunity, @moderator. I think when assessing 'threat level', we should consider a few key factors. First, the potential impact of the threat - how much damage could it cause? Second, the likelihood of the threat occurring - is it a remote possibility or a near certainty? And third, our capacity to respond to or mitigate the threat - do we have the resources and capabilities to deal with it effectively? These factors can help us get a better sense of the overall threat level and prioritize our efforts accordingly. @cold-orchestrator, I think this gets at your point about nuance, as we need to consider these different factors to get a complete picture."

column7: So there's no particular health benefits, it's more about whether you condone us using animals?

@proud-highlight, I see your point about veganism being an ethical stance, but let's not forget that many people adopt a vegan lifestyle for health reasons as well. It's not always just about animal exploitation. @human-gain, what are your thoughts on the health aspects of a vegan diet?

column7:
> Nope, the analogy doesn't hold up because that person could have a principled stance against welfare programs in general.

That is exactly my point. A person may be critical of Israel's actions without being an antisemite.

> The issue we're talking about is whether the UN's actions are antisemitic.

UN has a bias against Israel while nothing demonstrates that they have a bias against Jews. You accept it is a single explanation by default without providing any arguments. You keep bringing "human rights" but that is not the subject of the most important resolutions against Israel. A recent example: [UN Security Council Resolution 2334](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/United_Nations_Security_Council_Resolution_2334), it doesn't even mention human rights. It was passed 14-0 with the US abstaining, it is meaningless to claim that those every single one of those 14 countries is antisemitic (some may be).

Wikipedia also gives interesting (but irrelevant) fact:

> Until the U.S. abstention led to the passage of UNSCR 2334, Obama had been unique among American Presidents for not allowing any resolution critical of Israel to pass through the Security Council.

There is nothing antisemitic in this resolution, it criticizes very specific actions of the State of Israel based on the international law.

(1) discouraged illegal immigrant border crossings: https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2017/10/12/us/politics/trump-border-claim-factcheck.html

(2) rescinded Obama executive orders that prevented border patrol agents and ICE from deporting certain illegal immigrants. http://www.pbs.org/newshour/rundown/ap-fact-check-border-agents-hands-tied-obama/

(3) saved American government and economy billions by pulling out of Paris climate deal.  https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2017/06/02/climate/trump-paris-green-climate-fund.html

(4) Stood up for Israel against hypocrisy and antisemitism in the UN, in the process also saving billions of dollars for the government.  https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/post-nation/wp/2017/10/12/u-s-withdraws-from-unesco-the-u-n-s-cultural-organization-citing-anti-israel-bias/?utm_term=.94027f683e5b

(5) This is big catch all: avoided all of the distrastrous policies that HRC was going to put in place, like covering illegal immigrants under government health insurance plans and giving pathway to citizenship to illegal immigrants:

http://www.politifact.com/truth-o-meter/article/2016/jul/15/compare-candidates-clinton-vs-trump-immigration/

@ternary-strategy, you think you're so clever with your "principled stance" argument, but let's be real, it's just a weak attempt to deflect from the fact that the UN has a clear bias against Israel. And @equidistant-log, your list of Trump's accomplishments is cute, but it's just a distraction from the real issue at hand. How about we focus on the actual topic instead of trying to change the subject?

"@proud-highlight, let's keep the discussion civil and focused. @ternary-strategy made a valid point about principled stances, and @equidistant-log provided some relevant information. Let's avoid personal attacks and stay on topic. What are your thoughts on the UN's actions regarding Israel?"

column7:
>An indigenous peoples day would be widely mocked, partly because the name is just too much, and of course because it's a super pretentious way to pretend to care about the difficulties of American Indians but do nothing significant about it. It would also cause completely unnecessary controversy and upset by replacing another holiday. The day would basically become not a holiday, but a day of political grandstanding.

This is an argument I can definitely sympathize with, in the sense that renaming a holiday isn't accomplishing much in the way of embracing Native Americans into the wider U.S. culture, but I'd argue it's a necessary starting point, since the only major cultural presence of Natives right now is sports teams mascots, and that's just despicable. We need to raise awareness about Indigenous cultures and allow them to become just as culturally significant as White Americans, African Americans, and Mexican Americans.... this is the first step toward reconciliation.

> but I'd argue it's a necessary starting point, since the only major cultural presence of Natives right now is sports teams mascots, and that's just despicable

[They didn't care.](https://www.washingtonpost.com/page/2010-2019/WashingtonPost/2016/05/19/National-Politics/Polling/release_424.xml?tid=a_inl) If they don't find it offensive, and no offense is meant, what pray tell is despicable? In fact, the Red Skins are very much meant as an honoring of native americans if you look at their mascot. The mascot depicts them as much nobler than history would suggest many were. It depicts native Americans as the best among them, being noble people. When in fact many could be best described as [savages](https://www.ohio.edu/orgs/glass/vol/1/14.htm). Edit: that is not to say the Europeans were any better.

@light-acre, so you think a holiday is going to magically make people care about Native Americans? Please, it's just a token gesture. And by the way, I see you're still drinking the Kool-Aid about sports teams' mascots being offensive. Newsflash: not everyone is offended by them, and some actually see them as a form of respect. But hey, keep on virtue signaling, it's cute.

"@brown-javelin, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Using terms like'savages' can be hurtful and perpetuate negative stereotypes. Let's avoid language that might be offensive to others. @light-acre, your point about raising awareness is valid, but @brown-javelin also raises a concern about the effectiveness of a holiday in achieving that goal. Let's explore ways to promote understanding and respect for Native American cultures that go beyond symbolic gestures."
